segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an intro music track playing over a title card that reads 'THE ANCIENT REMEDY OF DIABETES REVEALED' and 'INTENSIVE DIETARY MANAGEMENT DR JASON FUNG' against a red curtain. The scene then transitions to a well-lit lecture hall where Dr. Jason Fung, a man wearing glasses and a light-colored button-up shirt, stands in front of a projection screen. He begins speaking, explaining that "diabetes they tell you is a chronic disease" and that "this is what happens in all studies" with medications. He gestures with his hands, emphasizing how sugars "start out actually not bad" and initially come down with medication, but "over the years, it kind of the effect sort of wears off," leading to the need for "more and more and more medications." The screen initially displays a graph titled 'It is Hard to Treat T2DM Adequately... Because T2DM is a Progressive Disease' from the ADOPT study, illustrating rising HbA1c levels over time. Dr. Fung continues to explain that "no matter what drug you take," such as "Metformin, Glyburide, and Rosiglitazone," the initial effect is "very good up to about six months," but then "the effect kind of wears off," and "the sugar slowly goes up." He uses hand movements to illustrate this progression, as the slide changes to a diagram titled 'Diabetes is getting worse!' showing the escalating treatment regimen: Metformin → Metformin + Glyburide → Insulin → More Insulin, with a large blue arrow labeled 'Over Time'. He concludes that this leads to needing "a second medication or a third medication," which "is pretty well what happens to everybody" who has had diabetes for a while. The audience is partially visible in the foreground, and bookshelves filled with binders are seen to the right, contributing to the academic atmosphere. A text overlay at the bottom reads 'For more information www.kidneylifescience.ca'.

segments.integrated_caption: Dr. Jason Fung, wearing a light-colored button-up shirt and glasses with a small microphone clipped to his shirt, stands before a projection screen, actively gesturing with both hands as he delivers a lecture on diabetes. He articulately explains that while the symptom is high blood sugar, current treatments are solely directed against it, failing to address the underlying disease. The slide behind him, titled 'Diabetes', visually reinforces this point with a table highlighting red-circled '????' for the unknown cause and treatment of 'Diabetes Insulin Resistance', contrasting it with 'Infection' where cause ('Bacteria') and treatment ('Antibiotic') are known. Dr. Fung emphasizes that controlling high blood sugars "doesn't do anything for you because you're not actually treating the disease," which "continues to progress." He points out the 'Symptomatic Treatment' of 'Insulin' and 'OHA' on the slide, noting with a green arrow that it "Does not alter disease process." He recounts how, up until about five years ago, the common belief was that treating sugars would improve the patient's condition, a misconception he believes many in the partially visible audience have heard. He then reveals findings from three large studies conducted around five years ago, which showed that whether sugars were well-controlled or poorly controlled, it made no difference to the progression of diabetes complications such as heart disease, strokes, eye disease, and kidney disease, none of which were made better by symptomatic treatment. Bookshelves filled with white binders are visible to the right, contributing to the professional academic setting.

segments.integrated_caption: Dr. Jason Fung, wearing a light-colored button-up shirt and glasses, stands in front of a brightly lit projection screen, actively lecturing on insulin resistance. He gestures with both hands, emphasizing points, and occasionally points toward the screen, which displays a comparative diagram titled 'What causes Insulin Resistance?' illustrating 'Normal Insulin Metabolism' versus 'Insulin Resistance' with details on glucose uptake and intracellular glucose levels. Partially visible text at the bottom reads 'Insulin causes insulin resistance'. Dr. Fung's clear, articulate voice explains that like any hormone or addictive substance such as marijuana, cocaine, Valium, or anti-psychotics, the body develops resistance to it with repeated exposure. He elaborates that while the first dose might yield a 'real high,' every subsequent one is 'a little bit lower,' requiring higher and higher doses to get the same effect, defining this as resistance or tolerance. He then directly applies this principle to insulin, stating, 'Insulin is exactly the same.' He describes a 'vicious cycle' where if insulin levels are high, the body develops insulin resistance, which in turn leads to even higher insulin levels. The tops of audience members' heads are partially visible in the foreground, indicating their attentive presence, while bookshelves filled with white binders to the right reinforce the academic and professional setting.

segments.integrated_caption: Dr. Jason Fung, wearing a light-colored button-up shirt and glasses, stands dynamically before a projection screen displaying a slide titled 'Current Treatment Paradigm: Idiotic'. He gestures expressively with both hands, emphasizing his critique of current diabetes treatments. He draws an analogy in a clear, articulate voice, stating, "treating an alcoholic with alcohol... This is exactly the same thing," explaining how taking alcohol initially provides a "buzz" but subsequent doses are "a little lower," mirroring the concept of increasing resistance. He clarifies, "you're not making the alcoholism any better," directly relating to the slide's prominent text: 'You can’t treat a hyperinsulinemic state with more insulin!'. Dr. Fung asserts, "The problem is that your insulin levels are too high," and rhetorically asks, "So how can taking more insulin possibly be good for you?" He emphatically declares, "It was never good for you. In fact, it made things worse," aligning with the slide's graph showing a negative correlation between insulin dose and glucose disposal rate, marked with "Increasing insulin resistance!". He criticizes the medical community for not understanding this, leading them to label diabetes as "a chronic progressive disease." However, he refutes this, stating, "diabetes was not a chronic progressive disease because we know that we can cure it in many cases," before beginning to discuss solutions like "weight loss surgery or gastric" procedures. Partially visible audience members remain engaged in the foreground, while bookshelves filled with white binders maintain the professional academic atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: Dr. Jason Fung, wearing a light-colored button-up shirt and glasses, stands dynamically in front of a projection screen displaying a slide titled 'Treatment Considerations'. He criticizes past approaches where "we've prescribed you drugs that suppress the symptoms," which he states "is not gonna make it better, and it hasn't," adding that "The last 30 years has borne it out." Dr. Fung continues, stating that diabetes "is not a chronic progressive disease at all," but "is in fact a curable disease, but you have to treat it the right way." He explains that his clinic focuses on "ways of how to lower the insulin levels," identifying "intermittent fasting" as "one of the simpler ways to do it," which is why "we have a lot of people on these sort of protocols." He notes that "fasting has been done for many, many years," despite common misconceptions. Partially visible audience members are in the foreground, and bookshelves filled with white binders are to the right of the screen, maintaining the academic setting.

segments.integrated_caption: Dr. Jason Fung, wearing glasses and a light-colored button-up shirt, dynamically lectures in front of a Projection Screen displaying the 'Hormonal Obesity Theory' diagram. He actively gestures with his hands, explaining how "very low carbohydrate diets" and "high fat diet" relate to health, addressing the common concern, "isn't that gonna clog up my arteries?" He points to elements on the slide as he elaborates on the "Hormonal Obesity Theory," emphasizing that the "real key is the insulin levels." He details how "insulin levels" are protective factors, but a "vicious cycle between insulin and insulin resistance" is fueled by "fructose, carbohydrates, wheat," which all contribute to the "high insulin diet that we all eat." Dr. Fung connects this to the "obesity crisis" and "diabetes crisis," explaining that "processing is what's really bad 'cause it strips out the fat and strips out the fiber," removing "major protective factors" and leading to "increased insulin levels." He states that "insulin leads not only to the weight," while occasionally adjusting his glasses and touching his head. The academic setting is reinforced by Bookshelves filled with white binders to his right, and an On-Screen Text Overlay 'More information www.kidneylifescience.ca' provides a resource at the bottom.
